output_summary: aligned schemas for datasets: amazon_small, goodreads_small, metabooks_small; generated 2 schema correspondence groups.
profiled datasets: amazon_small, goodreads_small, metabooks_small.
Reused the blocking configuration from state.
Blocking uses `semantic_similarity` for goodreads_small_amazon_small, metabooks_small_amazon_small, metabooks_small_goodreads_small, blocking on title, author, publish_year.
Performance-wise, pair completeness ranges from 0.99 to 1.00 and candidate counts range from 149824 to 199823.
The config also records dataset id columns.

matcher mode `rule_based` with config source reused from state; evaluated pairs: id_columns, matching_strategies; selected scores: matching_strategies (threshold=0.66, f1=0.8029330889092575, threshold=0.72, f1=0.852589641434263, threshold=0.68); produced 2 pair strategy entries.
This run was an initial draft of the pipeline.

The pipeline was created from scratch to load the three parquet datasets, align schemas by renaming Goodreads and Metabooks columns to the Amazon schema using an LLM-based schema matcher, perform pairwise entity matching, and run data fusion on the combined correspondences.
The matching stage uses three preconfigured EmbeddingBlockers for Goodreads-to-Amazon, Metabooks-to-Amazon, and Metabooks-to-Goodreads candidate generation.
The entity matching stage uses RuleBasedMatcher with pair-specific comparator sets, weights, and thresholds for the three dataset pairs.
The fusion stage defines attribute-level fusers for 12 fields and writes the fused result to output/data_fusion/fusion_data.csv along with correspondence CSV files for each pair.
Fusion behavior is centered on longest_string as the dominant strategy for textual attributes such as title, author, publisher, language, bookformat, and edition.
The strategy uses maximum for numeric or score-like fields including publish_year, rating, numratings, page_count, and price.
The strategy uses union specifically for genres, shifting multivalue combination toward set-style aggregation rather than selecting a single source value.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an initial draft that generated a new evaluation script for the current fused output.

The evaluation used schema-specific matching with tokenized comparison for text fields, set equality for list-like fields, numeric tolerance for numeric fields, and an eval view that remapped fused rows to gold IDs before scoring.
The script grouped title, author, language, and publisher under tokenized_match.
The script evaluated genres and any detected list-like non-underscore columns with set_equality_match after normalization.
The script evaluated publish_year and page_count with numeric_tolerance_match.

The run added build_eval_view to expand fused rows by parsed _fusion_sources, prefer source IDs matching the detected gold prefix or explicit gold IDs, and fall back to _id when no mapping candidates were found.
The run added _parse_source_ids using json.loads and ast.literal_eval to tolerate multiple serialized source-list formats.
The run added _detect_gold_prefix to infer the dominant gold ID prefix from the gold dataset.
The run added list-like column detection and normalization across fused_eval and gold, excluding identifier and internal confidence columns.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 62.14%; evaluation attempt counter is 1.
The run is failing mainly in fusion rather than blocking or matching.
Fusion is dropping or failing to surface some gold entities, which causes repeated missing fused values across multiple attributes for the same IDs.
The fusion rules are also selecting wrong attribute values within matched clusters, especially for author, title, publisher, publish_year, page_count, and genres.
Normalization is insufficient for author, title, publisher, and genres, which amplifies fusion errors.
Overall accuracy is 0.6214285714285714 across 140 evaluations with 87 correct values.

Author accuracy is 0.45, title accuracy is 0.65, publisher accuracy is 0.65, and genres accuracy is 0.05, while language accuracy is 0.9, page_count accuracy is 0.8, and publish_year accuracy is 0.85.

The same gold IDs have multiple missing attributes, including amazon_135479 missing author, language, page_count, title, genres, publish_year, and publisher, and amazon_12422 missing author, language, page_count, title, genres, and publish_year.
The pipeline code sets include_singletons=False in DataFusionEngine.run, which can remove unmatched records from fused output.
This run was an evaluation-driven refinement of the existing pipeline.
The fusion stage was revised to keep singleton records by changing include_singletons from False to True.
The attribute fusers for title, author, publisher, language, bookformat, publish_year, and page_count were changed to voting.
The edition fuser was changed from longest_string to shortest_string.
Pre-fusion preprocessing was added to normalize title, author, publisher, language, and genres fields across all datasets.
Post-fusion cleanup was added to re-normalize fused genres, title, author, and publisher values.
The pipeline shifted away from longest-string and maximum-heavy fusion toward voting-based fusion for most bibliographic attributes.
Fusion behavior now favors normalized consensus values instead of verbose variants, later years, or larger page counts.
Singleton retention changed the output behavior from dropping unmatched records to preserving them in the fused result.
Blocking configurations and matching thresholds were left unchanged in this run.
New helper functions were introduced for missing-value detection, HTML unescaping, whitespace cleanup, and field-specific normalization.
Title normalization removes trailing parenthetical qualifiers.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was a refinement of the evaluation configuration in evaluation_refinement mode rather than an initial draft or repair.

The dominant strategy was to keep the gold-ID-aligned fused evaluation view and list normalization flow while refining field-specific evaluation functions, especially changing publish_year from numeric_tolerance_match to year_only_match.
The run also emphasized clearer structured evaluation reporting through a new print_metrics helper without changing the core fused-to-gold alignment logic.
Title, author, language, and publisher continued to use tokenized_match as a text-field evaluation family.
Genres remained grouped under set-based comparison using set_equality_match, including automatic registration for other detected list-like columns.
Numeric-like fields were split so that publish_year used year_only_match while page_count continued to use numeric_tolerance_match.
The import block was expanded to include year_only_match alongside the existing evaluation helpers.
A new print_metrics helper was added to emit compact overall, per-attribute, and error-summary diagnostics from the evaluation results.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 65.71%; previous logged accuracy was 62.14%; evaluation attempt counter is 2.
The run is primarily failing in fusion logic and preprocessing rather than blocking.
Two gold Amazon entities are completely missing from the evaluated fused output, creating systematic coverage errors.
For records that do exist, several attribute fusers are selecting poor fused values or collapsing useful bibliographic information.
Overall accuracy is 0.6571428571428571 across 140 evaluations, with 92 correct values.
Two gold ids, amazon_135479 and amazon_12422, are missing for all 7 evaluated attributes, contributing 14 of 48 mismatches.
The missing-record cases show fused_id = NaN and inputs = [], which indicates a coverage or ID-lineage problem rather than a bad attribute choice.
Genres accuracy is 0.05, which is the worst attribute and is consistent with the current use of union plus string flattening.
Title accuracy is 0.65, and the analysis reports many fused titles are shortened variants after normalization removes parenthetical qualifiers.
Publisher accuracy is 0.6, and the analysis reports fused publishers often choose imprint or alias variants instead of the expected canonical publisher.
Page_count accuracy is 0.65, and the analysis reports voting is unstable when page counts differ by edition.
This run was a refinement in evaluation_refinement mode driven by the prior error analysis.
The fusion strategy was reworked so that title, author, publisher, bookformat, and edition now use longest_string instead of voting or shortest_string.
Numeric fusion was changed so that publish_year and page_count use choose_consensus_numeric, while rating, numratings, and price use choose_max_numeric.
Genres fusion was changed from union to choose_best_genres, and language fusion was changed from voting to choose_best_language.

The preprocessing was revised to preserve more information by separating title normalization for matching from title_core creation, retaining full author strings for output-oriented handling, converting genres into lists, and coercing publish_year and page_count to integers.
The prepared datasets now add a _source field and define a source_priority map for source-aware fusion behavior.
The pipeline shifted away from generic voting and union toward completeness-based and attribute-specific fusion rules.
Fusion now prefers richer bibliographic strings via longest_string for key text attributes rather than majority variants that can shorten titles or drop author and publisher detail.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or repair.
The dominant strategy was to refine evaluation alignment and comparison behavior by keeping the gold-id mapping view, normalizing list-like columns, and adjusting field-specific evaluation functions.

The only substantive evaluation-function change in this run was switching publish_year from year_only_match to numeric_tolerance_match while retaining tokenized matching for title, author, publisher, and language and set equality for genres.
Title, author, publisher, and language were evaluated as tokenized text fields with tokenized_match.
Genres was treated as a list-like set field and evaluated with set_equality_match after list normalization.
Publish_year and page_count were grouped as numeric-style fields and evaluated with numeric_tolerance_match in the refined script.
Identifier and coverage behavior was handled through the build_eval_view mapping that expands _fusion_sources into eval_id values aligned to gold ids.
The run preserved the build_eval_view helper that maps fused rows to gold-aligned eval_id values using parsed _fusion_sources and cluster fallback behavior.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
